title: Wooden Christmas Tree Candle Holders
summary: Small stacked-layer wood trees with a mitered 30° edge on each layer, built in two sizes — a fast holiday scrap-wood project using 1x6 or 1x4 offcuts.
description:
These little trees get their triangular shape from a single repeated cut: each layer is mitered at 30° off square on both ends, long point measurement, with the ends deliberately not parallel — the same angle repeated at three different lengths per tree to build the tapering silhouette. Attach the base to the vertical back support first, extending the base off the edge of your workbench so you can lay the whole project flat while nailing — a small but genuinely useful positioning trick for a piece this size.

The bottom (widest) tree layer goes on next, centered on the base and nailed from both the front and the bottom for a secure hold, then glue goes between each subsequent layer as it stacks, with the back support anchoring every layer from behind. **Two full sizes are included** — a larger version built from 1x6 stock and a smaller one from 1x4 — and the cut list, layer count, and back support length all scale down together for the small version, so pick one size and stick to its full cut list rather than mixing dimensions between the two.

This is a genuinely fast build with minimal material, good for a small pile of pine or cedar offcuts, and works equally well as a candle holder centerpiece or a simple decorative shelf accent.

materials.name: 1x6, 4 ft (large tree, or cedar fence picket)
Primer and paint, or stain and poly

steps.title: Cut one tree's full parts list
steps.body: Pick one size and cut its whole list — the two versions share no parts and mixing them will not stack. For the large tree cut from the 1x6 a base at 6-1/4" and three layers at 15-3/8", 11" and 6-3/8", each with both ends mitered 30° off square at the long-point measurement and both miters running the same way, so each layer comes out a wide shallow wedge with its long edge at the bottom; cut the 1x2 back at 14-1/2". The small tree is the same sequence from the 1x4 — base 4-1/4", layers 10-1/8", 7-1/8" and 4", back 9-1/2" — but note those four 1x4 pieces total 25-1/2" against the 2 ft board the material list supplies, so you will need a little extra stock. Set the miter saw to 30° once and leave it there; every angled cut on the tree is that one setting, just flipped end for end.
materials: 1x6, 4 ft (large tree, or cedar fence picket)

steps.title: Attach the back to the base
steps.body: Stand the 1x2 back on edge, centered on the back edge of the base, and glue it down, then drive 1-1/4" brads up through the underside of the base into it — driving from below is what gives the nail enough length to bury itself, since the base is only 3/4" thick. Check with a square that the back stands upright to the base in both directions before the glue sets: every layer above registers off this piece, so a back that leans puts the whole tree out of plumb. Let the base hang off the edge of your bench so the assembly lies flat while you work.
materials: 1x6, 4 ft (large tree, or cedar fence picket)

steps.title: Attach the bottom layer
steps.body: Center the widest layer on the base against the front face of the back piece — 15-3/8" for the large tree, 10-1/8" for the small — long-point edge down, so the mitered ends splay outward and the layer reads as the tree's bottom branches. Glue it to both the base and the back, then drive two 1-1/4" brads from the front into the back piece and two up through the underside of the base. Measure the overhang at each end to confirm it is centered before the glue grabs; on a piece this small, an eighth of an inch off-center is visible across a room.
materials: 1x6, 4 ft (large tree, or cedar fence picket)

steps.title: Add the middle and top layers
steps.body: Stack the middle layer on the bottom one and the top layer on that, gluing each layer's lower edge and its contact with the back piece. Nail each one with two 1-1/4" brads driven through the back piece from behind, so no nail hole shows on the face of the tree. Look down the front from above as you go: each layer's long-point edge should be centered on the one below, and the mitered ends should line up into two straight sloping edges from base to tip — that pair of lines is the whole silhouette.
materials: 1x6, 4 ft (large tree, or cedar fence picket)

steps.title: Sand and finish
steps.body: Sand the faces and lightly ease the mitered points, which are fragile short grain and will splinter if left sharp. Fill the nail holes in the base if you plan to paint, then prime and paint, or stain and seal with poly. Nothing in this build is a candle cup — the base is a flat wooden shelf — so set only a candle already in its own holder on it, and keep any flame clear of the layers above.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

images.alt: Small stacked-layer wood Christmas tree candle holders in two sizes
